title: Synthetic Polariton Matter in the solid state
abstract: Synthetic materials are obtained by assembling atoms or artificial atoms into regular arrays, thereby forming artificial crystals that offer powerful platforms to emulate and explore condensed-matter phenomena in highly controlled settings. They enable probing outstanding questions in many-body physics and designing new phases of matter with no direct analogue in nature. Beyond their fundamental interest, these materials hold potential for future technological applications through the emergence of novel concepts and functionalities. Synthetic materials have been engineered using a wide range of physical platforms, including both natural atoms and fabricated artificial atoms in the solid-state. A particularly intriguing approach relies on photons. When confined in optical cavities and strongly coupled to matter excitations, photons acquire an effective mass and can experience interactions, giving rise to hybrid light-matter quasiparticles known as polaritons. By arranging polaritons in periodic structures, one can engineer synthetic photonic materials with tailored band structures and controllable interactions, offering a promising route toward exploring strongly correlated photonic phases. This chapter focuses on a solid-state realization of such systems: exciton polaritons confined in semiconductor microcavities. Following a general introduction, we describe how photon mass and photonic band structures emerge from cavity confinement, and how interactions arise via strong coupling to excitons in quantum wells. We finally review how these ingredients can be used to explore rich physics from the mean-field to the quantum regime.
summary: This paper reviews the development of synthetic polariton matter using semiconductor microcavities. By confining photons in cavities and coupling them to excitons, researchers can create hybrid quasiparticles with controllable mass and interactions. These particles can be arranged into artificial crystals to simulate complex quantum models like the Bose-Hubbard model. This platform offers a scalable way to explore strongly correlated bosonic phases and topological physics in a driven-dissipative environment.
main_problem: The challenge of engineering and controlling new phases of matter, such as strongly correlated bosonic phases, by creating synthetic photonic materials in the solid state.
main_result: The paper demonstrates that exciton-polaritons in semiconductor microcavities can be arranged into periodic arrays to emulate the Bose-Hubbard model, providing a scalable platform for exploring many-body quantum physics.
method: The work uses a combination of electromagnetic modeling (Maxwell equations), tight-binding approximations (LCAO), and the driven-dissipative Gross-Pitaevskii equation to describe both single-particle and many-body dynamics.
model_or_system: The primary system is exciton-polaritons in semiconductor microcavities (micropillars) which, when arranged in lattices, map onto the Bose-Hubbard model and the Jaynes-Cummings model.
key_observables: Photoluminescence signal, energy-momentum dispersion, photon statistics (second-order correlation function g2(0)), and spatial/temporal correlation functions.
important_parameters: Effective photon mass (m_ph), hopping amplitude (J), on-site interaction (U), Rabi splitting (Omega), and the ratio of interaction to linewidth (U/gamma).
main_assumptions: The paraxial approximation for photon dispersion, the weak-guidance approximation for electromagnetic fields, and the treatment of excitons as bosons at low density.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of synthetic photonic materials and the mapping to the Bose-Hubbard model; Figure 2 illustrates the geometrical optics and derivation of effective photon mass; Figure 7 and 8 show semiconductor microcavity structures and exciton formation.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from the fundamental physics of photon confinement and effective mass to the engineering of periodic lattices, then explores the transition from linear single-particle physics to nonlinear many-body regimes and emergent quantum phases.
why_interesting: It presents a scalable, solid-state alternative to cold atom platforms for studying driven-dissipative many-body physics, including Bose-Hubbard dynamics, topological phases, and non-equilibrium phase transitions like KPZ physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Schematic illustration of a synthetic photonic material composed of an array of coupled optical cavities. (a) Neighboring cavities are coupled with strength 𝐽, while each cavity contains a nonlinear medium that induces an effective photon–photon interaction 𝑈. (b) The system maps onto a Bose–Hubbard model with hopping amplitude 𝐽and on-site interaction 𝑈.

Fig. 2 Geometrical optics illustration of a planar Fabry—Perot cavity. (a) A light ray entering the cavity undergoes multiple reflections between the mirrors before escaping, during which it experiences a transverse drift that depends on the in-plane wavevector k∥. (b) This transverse motion is equivalent to that of a massive particle, as reflected in the cavity photon dispersion relation: the energy varies quadratically with k∥ , providing the confined photons with an effective mass.

Fig. 3 Photon confinement in semiconductor microcavities. (a) Schematic of a Bragg cavity composed of two distributed Bragg reflectors (grey) and a central cavity spacer (green). Using nanofabrication techniques, lateral microstructures can be defined, typically a few microns in diameter. (b) Calculated energy spectrum for a 3 µm micropillar, obtained by numerically solving the full three-dimensional Maxwell equations for a structure containing 15 Bragg mirror pairs in both the top and bottom DBR, and a cavity spacer of optical thickness 𝜆0 (design wavelength 𝜆0 = 852 nm). A series of discrete modes emerges, labeled according to the hybrid electric (HE𝑛𝑚) modes of a cylindrical waveguide....

Fig. 4 A regular lattice of polariton micropillars. (a) Scanning electron microscope image of a honeycomb lattice of polariton micropillars. The inset shows a single micropillar used as a building block. (b) Schematic of the honeycomb lattice, highlighting the two sublattices 𝐴and 𝐵and the primitive lattice vectors a1 and a2.

Fig. 5 Experimental measurement of photonic band structures. (a) Schematic of the experimental setup: the Fourier-plane emission is imaged onto the entrance slit of a spectrometer. (b) Scanning electron microscope image of the fabricated lattice. (c) Measured emission intensity as a function of 𝑘𝑥for 𝑘𝑦= 0. (d) By repeating the measurement for multiple values of 𝑘𝑦, the full three-dimensional band structure is reconstructed.

Fig. 6 Coupling of cavity photons to a single two-level system. (a) Schematic of the experimental setup. (b) Energy levels in the bare basis, with the coupling terms in the Hamiltonian indicated by magenta arrows. (c) Energy ladder in the normal-mode (dressed-state) basis, which becomes anharmonic as illustrated by the red arrows. (d) This anharmonicity gives rise to the photon blockade effect, resulting in photon antibunching.

Fig. 7 Quantum well excitons coupled to cavity photons. (a) Schematic of a micropillar cavity embedding a semiconductor quantum well at its center. (b) Bandgap engineering creates a confining potential for charge carriers. Under optical excitation (double arrow), an electron is promoted from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), leaving behind a hole. (c) The Coulomb attraction between the electron and the hole binds them into an exciton.

Fig. 8 Exciton-polaritons in a single mode cavity. (a) Schematic of a single mode cavity embedding a quantum well at its center. (b) Linear Hamiltonian energy levels in the bare basis, with the coupling terms indicated by magenta arrows. (c) Energy ladder in the polariton basis, for the linear Hamiltonian.
